title: Hacking Tinder
summary: A dive into how Tinder users game the algorithm and brand themselves.
showNotes: Part of the Supernova podcast series where Master of Media Design students investigate algorithms embedded in our everyday lives.
transcript:
Hello everyone, I'm Tara, and welcome to this episode of the Supernova podcast. Today, we will explore the fascinating world of Tinder, focusing on the techniques people use to bend its algorithm in their favor. A quick YouTube search for Tinder algorithm reveals a sea of tutorials promising to teach users how to hack the system. This reflects what Bandinelli and Gandini observed in their article titled Dating Apps, the Uncertainty of Marketized Love : "On the internet, a multiplicity of forums can be found whereby the most effective of these techniques are discussed, contrasted, and compared. And this can be as detailed as to what color to wear in your profile picture." As part of my research, I asked my guests if they were aware of such techniques. Mélanie and Gaëlle initially said they didn't know about these strategies. But after I explained, Mélanie shared her own observations:

"Pendant la première moitié de ma semaine, je n'avais vraiment aucune description. J'ai mis quatre photos zéro description. J'ai juste mis mode de communication en personne et c'est tout. Et ensuite, lors de la deuxième partie de ma semaine, j'ai j'ai rempli plus de critères à mon sujet. Et j'ai eu le sentiment que Tinder m'a récompensé . Il m'a proposé beaucoup plus de profils susceptible de me plaire beaucoup plus de profils similaires à nouveau parce que, à mon avis, l'algorithme a capté ce qui me plaisait"

For Mélanie, the algorithm seems to work through a system of reward: the more detailed information you provide, the more accurate your matches become. Diana, on the other hand, laughed at my question and openly admitted: "Yes." She explained:

"Personnellement, étant attirée par un certain physique, j'ai tendance à liker ces types de personnes-là, même s'ils ne me plaisent pas, pour me donner plus de chance de matcher avec des personnes qui pourraient me correspondre dans ce type-là."

Her approach highlights how some users train the algorithm to improve their experience, adapting their behavior strategically. Joh, however, had a more specific understanding of these techniques:

"Y'a des techniques qui sont très connues. Par exemple, quand arrive sur l'application et tu vois un profil si, dans les like tu vois que tu as un like, le deuxième profil que tu vas avoir, c'est forcément quelqu'un qui t'as liké. C'est de notoriété publique, c'est genre un pote qui m'a dit"

"Il y a la localisation. Tu sais que si tu mets que t'habites à Gland, et que la plupart des gens ont un périmètre de rencontre de 30 ou 40 kilomètres, tu vas pas rencontrer grand monde. Et du coup, c'est le fait de se placer dans certaines villes ou même où t'es pas."

In the article, we can read: "When testing with various types of opening messages, or profile pictures, individuals seem to be more occupied with developing strategies to adapt their cognitive, affective and behavioural habits to the dating app cultures and affordances."

This idea connects to another concept discussed in the article: personal branding. "In the market of dating apps, users need to communicate their value in the form of a brand, thus learning how to manage their brand as well as how to decipher the value of the brand of others."

According to the article, on Tinder, users become brands competing in a market. In the heterosexual dating sphere, this could be seen as two markets: men competing against men and women against women. Interactions between these markets look like business-to-business marketing, where each side showcases its "qualities" to attract the other.

In this not-so-imaginary marketplace, men and women's experiences differ greatly. As Joh mentioned:

"En tout cas en tant que gars, souvent tu vas peut-être avoir un like par semaine ou des fois un jour, tu sais pas pourquoi, t'en as 12. T'es vachement poussé à l'achat pour être vu. Là ou, y'a plein d'études qui le montre, quand t'es une meuf, même si tu mets une photo de plafond, en une heure, t'as des centaines de likes."

This disparity may stem from gender ratios on the app. According to Start.io, in 2022, Tinder users in Switzerland were 89.9% men and just over 10% women. To cope with this imbalance, many men turn to paid features.

In the book L'Amour sous Algorithme, Judith Duportail critiques this system, questioning whether it reinforces a superficial hierarchy based on attractiveness. Her rhetorical questions highlight the ethical issues underlying this system :

"It's a perfect crime. Has Tinder created an unfair algorithm that puts the ugly with the ugly and the beautiful with the beautiful? "

By understanding these mechanics, we see how Tinder leverages human insecurities for profit, offering users temporary escapes from the algorithm through paid features.

This brings us to the end of today's episode. We've explored how Tinder's algorithm influences user behavior. Through my interviews, it became clear that men and women navigate this app differently—reflecting not only the platform's algorithm but also broader societal norms.

This topic has many layers, from gamification to the monetization of loneliness, and I hope to dive deeper into these aspects in future episodes.

Thank you for listening to the SuperNova Podcast. Until next time, take care, swipe carefully !
